title: Fixing Inconsistent Type Inference for a Generic Math Calculator

statement.markdown: Write a TypeScript function `Calculator<T>` that takes a generic type `T` and performs basic arithmetic operations: addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division. The function should return the result of the last operation. However, the current implementation has a bug that causes type inference issues. Fix the bug to ensure the function works correctly for different types.
statement.inputFormat: The input will be two numbers and an operator (+, -, *, /).
statement.outputFormat: The function should return the result of the last operation.
statement.notes: Note that division by zero is not handled.

examples.explanation: Example usage of the Calculator function

hints.text: Use generics to ensure type safety

editorial.title: Editorial: Fixing Inconsistent Type Inference for a Generic Math Calculator
editorial.intuition: The bug arises from inconsistent type inference when performing arithmetic operations. We need to ensure that the types are correctly inferred for each operation.
editorial.bruteForce: One approach is to manually specify the types for each operation, but this can lead to boilerplate code and reduced flexibility.
editorial.betterSolution: A better solution is to use generics to ensure type safety and consistency across all operations.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution involves using generics to define the Calculator class and then using a union type to handle the different operations.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The proof of correctness involves demonstrating that the function works correctly for different types and operations.
editorial.dryRun: Step-by-step example run: (1) Define the Calculator class with generics, (2) Use a union type to handle the different operations, and (3) Test the function with different inputs.
